title: Electing a president
description: Practice your skills in this quiz on the processes of US presidential elections, including primaries, caucuses, party conventions, and the Electoral College

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is the LEAST important underlying factor affecting the outcome of a presidential election?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Candidate’s southern heritage
Growth rate of the economy leading up to the election
Length of time the president’s party has held the White House
Incumbency advantage phenomenon

assessment_items.question_content:
During the 2016 Democratic and Republican presidential nomination process, more than half the states used the closed primary or caucus instead of an open primary.

**Which of the following statements accurately explains why a majority of states chose either a closed primary or caucus over an open primary?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To encourage party unity and prevent members of other parties from voting to nominate weak candidates
To keep everyone's vote secret
To determine the slate of electors to vote on behalf of the state at the Electoral College
To encourage greater electoral participation by allowing all citizens to vote for their preferred candidate from a list of candidates of all parties

assessment_items.question_content:
Solange attended her political party’s precinct meeting to discuss the top presidential nominees of her party and select delegates for upcoming district and state conventions. After some intense lobbying, the registered party members separated to different sides of the room to designate and count how many supporters each candidate had.

**Which of the following accurately describes the term for this political activity?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Presidential caucus
Presidential rally
Open primary
Top-two primary

assessment_items.question_content:
Nomination contest type |  Average turnout rate 
- | - 
**Open (or Semi-Open Primary)** |  34.2% 
**Closed primary** |  25.6%
**Mixed (one party primary/other caucus)** |  19.2%
**Caucus** | 6.7%
 
Source: [US Elections Project](https://comm.ncsl.org/productfiles/83453552/McDonald_NCSL_2016.pdf)

**Which of the following statements is reflected in the data in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Registered voters are more likely to turn out for open primaries than for a caucus
Registered voters are more likely to turn out for a caucus than a closed primary
Registered voters are more likely to turn out for a general election than for a primary election
Registered voters are more likely to turn out for a general election than a midterm election

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is an accurate comparison of a closed primary and a caucus?**

 | Closed primary | Caucus
- | - | -
**A** | Used for both presidential and congressional elections | Only used for presidential elections 
**B** | Limited to registered members of a political party  | Party members choose nominees after hours of speeches and debates
**C** | Meeting of delegates from one political party to select party candidates for public office | Any person can participate and discussion is not limited to registered party members
**D** | Generally has the highest turnout of any primary election | Generally has the lowest turnout of any primary election

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Comparison A
Comparison B
Comparison C
Comparison D

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the text below to answer the following question. 

>"The Electors shall meet in their respective states and vote by ballot for President and Vice-President, one of whom, at least, shall not be an inhabitant of the same state with themselves…and if no person have such majority, then from the persons having the highest numbers not exceeding three on the list of those voted for as President, the House of Representatives shall choose immediately, by ballot, the President. But in choosing the President, the votes shall be taken by states, the representation from each state having one vote . . ."

Excerpt from Amendment XII of the US Constitution

**Which of the following statements would the constitutional founders most agree with in regards to this excerpt from the Twelfth Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: This indirect method prevents voters across the country from ignoring better qualified candidates outside their region, and allows less populous states greater influence in presidential selection
The indirect method shows how committed the new republic was to boosting the power of the national government by giving electors from each state the role of “kingmakers” in presidential selection
The Electoral College reflects a rejection of popular sovereignty by making the process indirect and convoluted, thus taking the national constituency out of the selection process
The Electoral College addresses a profound distrust of power residing with the chief executive, as this indirect electoral procedure preserves the superiority of the legislative branch

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the text below to answer the following question. 

>"The Electors shall meet in their respective states and vote by ballot for President and Vice-President, one of whom, at least, shall not be an inhabitant of the same state with themselves . . . and if no person have such majority, then from the persons having the highest numbers not exceeding three on the list of those voted for as President, the House of Representatives shall choose immediately, by ballot, the President. But in choosing the President, the votes shall be taken by states, the representation from each state having one vote . . ."

-Excerpt from Amendment XII of the US Constitution

**According to this Amendment, how is the president chosen if no candidate receives a majority of votes by the Electoral College?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The House of Representatives votes on the top three vote-getters, with each state having one vote
A joint body of senators and representatives will choose among the top three candidates that advance from the Electoral College vote
The Senate votes on the top three vote-getters, with each senator having one vote
Each of the representatives in the House gets one vote to determine which of the top three candidates should become president

assessment_items.question_content:
Presidential candidate |  Electoral Votes | Popular Votes 
- | - | - | - 
**Republican Party** |  51.8% | 46.9% 
**Democratic Party** |  48.2% | 48.5%
**Green Party ** |  0% | 3.2%
**Reform Party** | 0% | 1.4%

**Based on the results shown in the table, who won the election?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Republican Party
The Democratic Party
The Green Party
The Reform Party

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the table below to answer the following question. 

Candidate | Party | Electoral Votes | Popular Votes 
- | - | - | - 
**Trump** | Republican | 304 | 62,980,160 
**Clinton** | Democratic | 227 | 65,845,063
**Johnson** | Libertarian | 0 | 4,448,931
**Stein** | Green |0 | 1,457,050
**McMullin** | Independent | 0  | 728,830

Source: [270towin.com](https://www.270towin.com/2016_Election/)

Based on the election results table as well as the margin of victory map, the third-party candidates for president collected over 6.5 million popular votes altogether in the 2016 national election.

**Which best explains why none of the third-party candidates received any electoral votes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: None of them acquired more votes than any other candidate on the ballot in each state
None of them acquired a minimum of 20% of the popular vote nationwide to earn electoral votes
State electors refused to cast votes for third-party or independent candidates
Most states did not put third-party candidates on the presidential ballot

assessment_items.question_content:
Presidential candidate |  Electoral Votes | Popular Votes 
- | - | - | - 
**Republican Party** |  51.8% | 46.9% 
**Democratic Party** |  48.2% | 48.5%
**Green Party ** |  0% | 3.2%
**Reform Party** | 0% | 1.4%

**Based on the data shown in the table, which of the following statements is true about the Electoral College?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The person who is elected to office may win the majority of Electoral College votes, but lose the popular vote
The person who is elected to office may win the majority of popular votes, but lose the Electoral College vote
The Electoral College gives independents and third-party candidates an unfair advantage in presidential elections
The Electoral College does not determine who wins the election, only the popular vote

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the table and map below to answer the following question. 

Candidate | Party | Electoral Votes | Popular Votes 
- | - | - | - 
**Trump** | Republican | 304 | 62,980,160 
**Clinton** | Democratic | 227 | 65,845,063
**Johnson** | Libertarian | 0 | 4,448,931
**Stein** | Green |0 | 1,457,050
**McMullin** | Independent | 0  | 728,830

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cedd1afb09f229e649c100b27032a1e4.png)

Source: [270towin.com](https://www.270towin.com/2016_Election/)

**Which aspect of the 2016 presidential election has fueled a debate about how democratic the constitutional process is for electing US presidents?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Even though Democrat Hillary Clinton outpolled Donald Trump by nearly 2.9 million popular votes, Trump won with a majority of electoral votes
The Green and Libertarian third-party candidates for president transferred all their popular votes to the Republican candidate
Maine allocated one of its electoral votes to Donald Trump instead of following the common procedure for vote allocation
A state-by-state recount of the popular vote changed the final tally based on accusations of corruption in the vote count

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements best explains how the winner-take-all allocation of Electoral College votes works?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The candidate who wins the popular vote in a state wins all of its Electoral College votes
The candidate who wins the popular vote in that state wins the proportionally equivalent amount of Electoral College votes for that state
The party that wins the popular vote in that state controls the redistricting of congressional districts in that state
The party who wins the popular vote in that state wins all open congressional seats in that state

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the images below to answer the following question. 

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fd95b9ffc9a57008f3851c140dec5c08.png)

Source: [270towin.com](https://www.270towin.com/2012_Election/)


![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cedd1afb09f229e649c100b27032a1e4.png)

Source: [270towin.com](https://www.270towin.com/2016_Election/)

Six states changed from Democrat to Republican between the 2012 and 2016 elections.

**Of these six states, which of the following pairs would have swung the 2016 election the other way if they had both voted Democrat?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Florida and Ohio
Pennsylvania and Wisconsin
Iowa and Michigan
Wisconsin and Michigan

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the image below to answer the following question. 

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cedd1afb09f229e649c100b27032a1e4.png)

>Source: [270towin.com](https://www.270towin.com/2016_Election/)

Florida, Iowa, New Hampshire, and Ohio frequently changed electoral support of presidential candidates from one major party to the other in elections between 1980 and 2016, with every presidential winner carrying at least three of these four states.

**What term describes these politically contentious areas of the country?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Perennial swing states
Gerrymandered districts
Realignment states
The blue wall

assessment_items.question_content:
A political strategist is advising a presidential campaign team during the general election season. The team has limited time and funding, so they must be strategic about where to campaign. The strategist argues that they should focus their efforts on swing states. They explain that winning swing states is often the key to securing enough electoral votes to win the presidency, even if the national popular vote is close. The candidate agrees and adjusts their travel and advertising plans to focus on these states.

**Which feature of the Electoral College system best explains this strategy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Most states use a winner-take-all method to allocate their electoral votes.
Each state is allotted a number of electors equal to its number of senators and representatives.
Smaller states receive too much attention due to their minimum of three electoral votes.
Some states allocate electoral votes by congressional district rather than by statewide results.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Public opinion on the method for electing the president**

| Year | Prefer Popular Vote | Prefer Electoral College |
|------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| 2000 | 61%                | 35%                     |
| 2004 | 61%                | 35%                     |
| 2008 | 62%                | 35%                     |
| 2012 | 62%                | 35%                     |
| 2016 | 51%                | 44%                     |
| 2020 | 60%                | 37%                     |
| 2024 | 63%                | 35%                     |
-Source: Pew Research Center surveys conducted between 2000 and 2024.

**Which statement best explains why the Electoral College has remained in place despite the trends shown in the data?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Public support for change has remained high, but amending the Constitution is a difficult and rarely used process.
The Constitution requires Congress to replace the Electoral College when public opposition rises above seventy percent.
State governments have voted not to abolish the Electoral College, even though public support for change is overwhelming.
Public support has shifted, but federal courts do not have the power to change the Electoral College system.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of these has most directly led to increased calls to reform the Electoral College?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Elections in which the electoral winner loses the popular vote
Growing concern over unequal representation among states
States shifting from winner-take-all to proportional voting system
Rising voter turnout in states that typically favor one political party
